title: BU president pauses signage policy after Pride flag controversy
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: BU President Melissa Gilliam pauses signage policy after protests over removal of Pride flags on campus.
Controversy began in August 2025 with requests to remove Pride flags, leading to rallies and a petition by March 2026.
Gilliam acknowledged impact on LGBTQ+ students and emphasizes further discussion on university policies and community values.

title: Red Sox complete Sonny Gray trade, send pitching prospect to Cardinals
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Red Sox complete trade by sending pitching prospect Patrick Galle to the Cardinals for pitcher Sonny Gray.
Galle, a right-handed pitcher drafted by Boston in 2025, has a fastball ranging from 95-99 mph.
Sonny Gray, 36, is a three-time All-Star with a 1-0 record and 4.50 ERA this season.

title: ‘The Real Housewives of Rhode Island’: Episode 2 recap
author: Lauren Daley
bullets: Episode 2 of 'The Real Housewives of Rhode Island' features drama-filled gatherings in Providence and Middletown.
Liz McGraw targets her step-daughter's relationship, preferring 'Gigi' over 'Grandma' to maintain a youthful image.
Tensions rise between cast members Jo-Ellen Tiberi and Rulla Nehme Pontarelli over rumors of infidelity and personal disputes.

title: Mass. House to vote on youth social media ban, strict school cellphone ban
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Massachusetts House to vote on banning social media for children under 14 and cellphone use in schools.
The proposed bill requires minors ages 14-15 to have parental consent to use social media platforms.
Quincy schools propose all-day cellphone ban using pouches to reduce distraction and enforce focus in class.

title: Maine father dies after saving his children from a rip current
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Ryan Jennings, 46, from North Yarmouth, Maine, died rescuing his children from a rip current at Juno Beach, Florida.
Jennings ensured his 12-year-old son and 9-year-old daughter survived; they were pulled to safety and hospitalized.
The Palm Beach County Fire Department stressed swimming at guarded beaches due to hidden hazards like rip currents.

title: He’s running the Boston Marathon to support neglected and at-risk kids
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Sean Meehan, 28, from Natick, is running the 2026 Boston Marathon for The Home For Little Wanderers.
The Home For Little Wanderers supports neglected and at-risk children with services like foster care and education.
Meehan joins others in raising awareness and funds as part of Boston.com's "Why I'm Running" series.

title: The Boston Marathon didn’t make this list of best marathons in America. Do you think it should have?
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Boston Marathon did not make USA Today's 2026 10Best Readers’ Choice Awards for best marathons in the U.S.
The Boston Marathon attracts 500,000 spectators and over 30,000 runners annually, known for its iconic Heartbreak Hill.
The Detroit Free Press Marathon topped the list, known for crossing international borders and unique themes.

title: Everything you need to know about camping at Massachusetts state parks this season
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: Massachusetts state parks' camping season runs from April to November, with some parks open only on weekends.
Reservations for DCR camping can be made up to four months in advance via Mass DCR Camping Reserve America or phone.
Camping costs range from $17 to $230 depending on location and accommodation, with different rates for in-state and out-of-state residents.

title: Roman Anthony: Red Sox 2-7 start is ‘unacceptable to the fans’
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: The Boston Red Sox start the season 2-7, marking their worst start since 2019.
Outfielder Roman Anthony calls the team's performance 'unacceptable' to both fans and their own standards.
The Red Sox rank 17th in batting average, tied for third-most errors, and their pitching staff has a 4.71 ERA.

title: Is this really Boston’s most annoying escalator?
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: A Reddit post criticized an often-broken escalator from Prudential skybridge to Copley Place, sparking debates among commuters.
Commenters nominated other escalators, including those at Porter Square, Courthouse Station, and Logan Airport's Terminal C as most frustrating.
Broken escalators can slow foot traffic and complicate commutes, especially for those with mobility issues or carrying luggage.
